front: What is an operating system?
back: A program that acts as an intermediary between user and hardware

front: What is virtualization?
back: A method that allows operating systems to run applications within other OS's

front: Does the bootstrap program tend to be simple or complex?
back: Simple

front: Where is the bootstrap program usually stored?
back: In the computer hardware in firmware

front: How does the distinction between kernel mode and user mode function as a rudimentary form of protection (security)?
back: It provides a form of security, in that certain instructions can not be executed unless in kernel mode. Similarly, hardware devices can only be accessed when the program is in kernel mode.

front: Traps (or exceptions) are another form of interrupts, and is software generated. Why are they caused?
back: Because of errors or by a specific request from a user program that an operating-system service be performed by executing a special operation called a system call.

front: What does multiprogramming do?
back: Increases CPU utilization, as well as keeping users satisfied by organizing programs so that the CPU always has one to execute.

front: What is the main difficulty that a programmer must overcome in writing an operating system for a real-time environment?
back: Keeping the operating system within the fixed time constraints of a real-time system.

front: Some CPUs provide for more than two modes of operation. What are two possible uses of these multiple modes?
back: Different kinds of user modes. Specific kernel modes, for example if you want a mode to only be able to use USB

front: Give two reasons why caches are useful. What problems do they solve? What problems do they cause? If a cache can be made as large as the device for which it is caching (for instance, a cache as large as a disk), why not make it that large and eliminate the device?
back: Caches are useful when two or more components need to exchange data, and the components perform transfers at differing speeds.

front: What are programs in execution called in a multiprogrammed system?
back: A process.

front: What loads the kernel into memory?
back: The bootstrap program

front: What does CPU executing check for between instructions?
back: interrupts

front: How does the controller inform the device driver that it has finished its operation?
back: via an interrupt

front: What do interrupts do?
back: alert the CPU to events that require attention

front: Where does the CPU load instructions from?
back: from memory

front: What is the one program running at all times on a computer called?
back: the kernel

front: What is a universally accepted definition of an operating system?
back: There is none

front: In what decade did Moore's Law predict that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit would double every 18 months?
What are programs associated with the operating system but are not necessarily part of the kernel?
back: system programs

front: The first program to run on power-on is what?
back: bootstrap program

front: How do clustered systems differ from multiprocessor systems? What is required for two machines belonging to a cluster to cooperate to provide a highly available service?
back: Clustered systems use messages to communicate, while processors in a multiprocessor system could communicate using shared memory

front: When does RAM lose its content?
back: when power is turned off or otherwise lost

front: What is a repository of quickly accessible data shared by the CPU and I/O devices?
back: Main memory

front: What is the main communication path between the major components of a computer system?
back: the system bus

front: What is a given computer architecture's native unit of data?
back: word

front: What is logging in an operating system?
back: Keeping track of which users use how much and what kinds of computer resources

front: Most computers don't have an instruction to move a bit, but do have one to move what?
back: a byte

front: What does a cluster consist of?
back: several computer systems connected via a network

front: What can clusters provide?
back: high-performance computing environments

front: What is parallelization?
back: diving a program into separate components that run in parallel on individual cores in a computer or computers in a cluster

front: What does WAN stand for?
back: wide-area network

front: What does the CPU do in multitasking systems?
back: executes multiple processes by switching among them

front: In what mode are instructions executed in the operating system?
back: kernel mode

front: When control is given to a user application, the mode is set to what?
back: user mode

front: The CPU reads instructions from what during the instruction-fetch cycle?
back: main memory

front: How many interrupts are generated per block?
back: one

front: What is the name of the program that initializes all aspects of a system?
back: bootstrap program

front: Emulation involves simulating what in software?
back: computer hardware

front: What are the main differences among the various storage systems?
back: speed, size, and volatility

front: How fast is emulation?
back: Very slow, usually

front: What do many homes use to protect their networks from security breaches?
back: firewall

front: What is a network operating system?
back: an operating system that provides features such as file sharing across the network

front: What kind of tree does Linux use as part of its CPU-scheduling algorithm?
back: a balanced binary search tree

front: What is a balanced binary search tree known as?
back: red-black tree

front: What is a bottleneck in a client-server system?
back: the server

front: Embedded systems almost always run what type of operating-system?
back: real-time operating systems

front: What makes it possible to run many operating systems on top of one core system?
back: virtualization

front: What is responsible for dynamically allocating and freeing memory space?
back: The operating system

front: How do users interact with the touch screen?
back: by making gestures

front: What is ABI?
back: application binary interface

front: What is a loader?
back: Program that loads the executable file into memory

front: What is a primary benefit of a layered operating system?
back: efficiency

front: What is the main advantage of the microkernel approach to system design?
back: efficiency

front: How many system calls do systems execute per second?
back: thousands

front: How do modern systems avoid linking libraries into executables?
back: Using dynamically linked libraries (DLLs in windows), which are loaded as needed

front: What are system calls?
back: Programming interface to the services provided by the OS

front: How are packets of information in predefined formats moved between processes by the operating system?
back: message passing

front: What does the CLI or command interpreter do?
back: Fetches a command from the user and executes it. Can be both built-in commands but also names of programs.

front: What is the main reason for choosing a GUI interface?
back: personal preference

front: What is tracing?
back: A method of collecting data for a specific event, such as steps involved in a system call invocation

front: What are shells?
back: Flavours of the CLI

front: What was the name of the interface that appeared with macOS in the 1980s?
back: Aqua

front: The Linux kernel is monolithic in that it runs entirely in what mode?
back: kernel

front: What is an approach to building OS that is similar to modules but more flexible? (Linux, Solaris etc does this)
back: Implementing loadbale kernel modules

front: What method of passing parameters is used if there are more than five parameters?
back: the block method

front: What are the three main methods of passing parameters in an operating system?
back:
1) Pass the parameters in registers (this may prove insufficient when there are more parameters than registers).
(2) Store the parameters in a block, or table, in memory, and pass the address of block as a parameter in a register. This approach is used by Linux and Solaris.
(3) Push the parameters onto a stack; to be popped off by the OS.

front: How does a programmer access a (system) API?
back: via a library of code provided by the operating system

front: What is UNIX structured similarly to?
back: monolithic structure

front: What does vmstat report?
back: memory-usage statistics

front: GRUB is an open-source bootstrap program for what two systems?
back: Linux and UNIX

front: What is CLI an abbreviation for?
back: Command line interface

front: What does an API specify?
back: a set of functions that are available to an application programmer

front: How are system calls usually accessed by programs?
back: Via a higher-level API rather than a direct system call use.

front: What is a hybrid kernel?
back: A kernel architecture based on combining aspects of microkernel and monolithic kernel architectures

front: What was Microsoft's first version of Windows based on?
back: MS-DOS operating system

front: Operating systems have CPU-scheduling routines that take into account what?
back: the speed of the CPU

front: What does the compiler generate in a machine- and operating-system specific language?
back: binaries

front: When did graphic user interfaces first appear?
back: early 1970s

front: What is the name of the file that OS’s generate that contain error information?
back: Log files

front: What allows maximum speed and convenience of communication, message passing or shared memory?
back: Shared memory

front: What is the Linux operating system based on?
back: UNIX

front: What has replaced the BIOS-based boot process?
back: UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface).

front: What is the name of the small boot loader located in nonvolatile firmware?
back: BIOS

front: Do Microkernels use message passing or shared memory for communication?
back: message passing

front: AOT compilation allows more efficient what?
back: application execution

front: Which is easier to implement, message passing or shared memory?
back: message passing

front: What was the original UNIX system's structure?
back: monolithic

front: What is a layered approach?
back: the operating system is broken into a number of layers (levels).

front: How can a system be made modular?
back: Using the layered approach

front: What are three ways to interact with an operating system?
back: (1) command interpreters, (2) graphical user interfaces, and (3) touchscreen interfaces

front: What language are most Android system libraries written in?
back: Java

front: What does macOS refer to as kernel extensions?
back: kexts

front: What’s so special about operating systems in the 1940s-1950s?
back: They didn’t exist. People interacted with the hardware directly.

front: What is a problem with passing system call parameters in registers?
back: In some cases there may be more parameters than registers

front: What does glibc stand for?
back: GNU C library

front: What is program execution?
back: The process of loading a program into memory and then running that program; details of program execution are handled by the OS

front: What is Instruction Set Architecture? (ISA)
back: Defines the set of machine language instruction that a computer can follow. The interface between the hardware and the software.

front: What language is the kernel of Android written mostly in?
back: C

front: What is the bottom layer of a layered operating system, what is the top?
back: The bottom is the hardware, the top is the user interface

front: What does languages like Python and Ruby do to be able to execute on multiple different systems? What does Java do?
back: Python and Ruby are interpreted languages, meaning they’re interpreted to native code, while Java uses a virtual machine.

front: How can you use stacks to pass system call parameters?
back: By pushing the parameters to a stack, which the OS will then pop when it needs them

front: What languages are often used for higher level routines?
back: C and C++

front: What is Applicatin Binary Interface? (ABI)
back: Defines a standard for binary portability across program. Defines system call interface to the OS and the hardware resources and sertvices available in the system through the user ISA.

front: What are flavors of the command-line interface called?
back: Shells

front: What is the purpose of the bootstrap loader?
back: It locates the kernel, loads it into memory, and starts it

front: Where is the bootstrap loader stored?
back: In ROM or EEPROM

front: What is an example of a common bootstrap loader that allows selection of kernel from multiple disks, versions, kernel options?
back: GRUB

front: What method does Linux and Solaris use to pass system call parameters to the OS?
back: The parameters are stored in a block, or table, in memory, and address of block passed as a parameter in a register

front: What does tracing do?
back: Collects data for a specific event, such as steps involved in a system call invocation

front: Ordinary pipes on Windows systems are termed what?
back: anonymous pipes

front: What is PCB an abbreviation of?
back: Process Control Block

front: What character can be used to construct a pipe on the command line?
What must the system do when the CPU switches form one process to another? (In relation to states)
back: When CPU switches to another process, the system must save the state of the old process and load the saved state for the new process via a context switch

front: What allows two processes on different machines to communicate over a network?
back: Sockets

front: What are the process states?
back: New → ready → running/waiting → terminated

front: What is cascading termination?
back: If a parent process is terminated, all related processes are terminated abnormally.

front: When does a context switch occur?
back: When the CPU switches from one process to another

front: What is a process?
back: A program in execution.

front: Which is invoked more often, short-term scheduler or long-term, and what does this mean?
back: Short-term. This means that short-term must be fast, while the long-term may be slow.

front: What is the system call to create a new process in UNIX?
back: fork()

front: How many registers are there?
back: The registers vary in number and type, depending on the computer architecture. They include accumulators, index registers, stack pointers, and general-purpose registers

front: What can be used to terminate a process in Linux and UNIX systems?
back: exit() system call

front: What is an orphan process?
back: An orphan process is a computer process whose parent process has finished or terminated, though it remains running itself.

front: What is the name of the state of the process that has finished execution?
back: Terminated

front: Why may mobile operating systems have to terminate existing processes to reclaim system resources?
back: resource constraints such as limited memory

front: What is a zombie process?
back: When no parent is waiting for the child to close, the process is a zombie.

front: What kind of system allows multiple threads to run in parallel?
back: multicore

front: How often does the CPU scheduler execute?
back: at least once every 100 milliseconds

front: What does Linux prefer to use instead of the term process?
back: task

front: What is a pipe?
back: a pipe is a technique for passing information from one program process to another. Ordinary pipes (not named) are unidirectional

front: What does the long-term scheduler (or job scheduler) do?
back: Select which processes should be brought into the ready queue (i.e. selects processes form a pool and loads them into memory for execution)

front: What is the system call the parent uses to wait for the child to complete? (in UNIX)
back: wait()

front: What does the short-term scheduler (or CPU scheduler) do?
back: Select which process should be executed next and allocates CPU. This is sometimes the only scheduler in a system.

front: What happens during the process state “new”?
back: The process is being created

front: What are processes generally identified and managed via?
back: A process identifier (pid)

front: What is the data section in a program?
back: The global variables

front: Are named pipes uni- or bidirectional?
back: Bidirectional

front: What is the heap section in a program?
back: Memory dynamically allocated during run time.

front: Are ordinary pipes uni- or bidirectional?
back: Unidirectional, the producer writes to one end, the consumer reads from the other end

front: What is the program counter?
back: The value that represents the current activity; next address of the next instruction to be executed.

front: What is the difference between a program and a process?
back: A program is a passive entity stored on a disk, a process is active.

front: Is non-blocking consídered synchronous or asynchronous?
back: Asynchronous

front: What happens during the process state “waiting”?
back: The process is waiting for some event to occur

front: What happens during the process state “ready”?
back: The process is waiting to be assigned to a processor

front: What happens during the process state “terminated”?
back: The process finished execution

front: What is the process control block?
back: Something that each process is represented by; many pieces of specific information associated with a specific process

front: What happens during the process state “running”?
back: The process has instructions which are being executed

front: What is the device queue?
back: A queue of all processes waiting for a particular I/O device

front: Is blocking considered synchronous or asynchronous?
back: Synchronous

front: What are named pipes?
back: Named pipes (aka FIFO) is an extension to ordinary pipes. A named pipe can last longer  than the lifetime of the process, and appears as a file and generally processes attach to it for interprocess communication.

front: What does IPC stand for?
back: interprocess communication

front: How many processes for one program?
back: At least one, but there can be several

front: What does the process scheduler do?
back: Selects an available process (possibly from a set of several available processes) for program execution on the CPU

front: What creates a child process?
back: a parent

front: What is the text section in a program?
back: The program code

front: When is a process independent?
back: If it cannot be affected or affect the other processes executing in the system

front: What kind of termination does this describe: If the parent is terminated, so are all the children?
back: Cascading termination

front: What is it called if both send and receive are blocking?
back: Rendezvous

front: List three common ways of mapping user threads to kernel threads
back:
One-to-one: maps one user-level thread to one kernel thread
Many-to-one: maps many user-level threads to one kernel thread
Many-to-many: maps many user-level threads to a smaller or equal number of kernel threads

front: What is parallelism?
back: When a system can perform more than one task simultaneously

front: What are signals used for in UNIX systems?
back: To notify a process that a particular event has occurred.

front: Can you have concurrency without parallelism?
back: Yes

front: What was designed to provide the illusion of parallelism by rapidly switching between processes?
back: CPU schedulers

front: What is the objective of Amdahl’s Law?
back: Identifying performance gains from adding additional cores to an application

front: What does TLS stand for?
back: thread-local storage

front: What are thread pools?
back: Pools where you create a number of threads where they await work

front: What is the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism?
back: Data parallelism distributes subsets of the same data across multiple cores and does the same operation on each, while task parallelism distributes threads across cores, with each thread performing a unique operation.

front: What are the different types of parallelism?
back: Data parallelism and Task parallelism

front: What is the one-to-one multithreading model?
back: Each user-level thread maps to kernel thread

front: What does task parallelism do?
back: Distribute threads across cores, each thread performing unique operation

front: What does data parallelism do?
back: Distribute subsets of the same data across multiple cores, same operation on each

front: Name three of the benefits of multithreading
back: Responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, scalability

front: What is a UNIX signal?
back: Signal used in UNIX systems to notify a process that a particular event has occurred

front: What is thread cancellation?
back: The task of terminating a thread before it has completed

front: What are pthreads?
back: The POSIX standard defining an API for thread creation and synchronization

front: What is the difference between a lightweight and a heavyweight process?
back: Threads are lightweight, programs (aka processes or tasks) are heavyweight

front: What are the three multithreading models?
back: Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many

front: What is the many-to-one multithreading model?
back: Many user-level threads mapped to single kernel thread

front: Provide at least three challenges when designing programming for multicore systems
back: Identifying tasks, balance, data splitting, data dependency

front: What is the many-to-many multithreading model?
back: Allows many user level thread to be mapped to many kernel threads

front: What is waiting time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: The amount of time a process has been waiting in the ready queue

front: What is the role of the dispatcher?
back: the module that gives control of the CPU's core to the process selected by the CPU scheduler

front: How does priority scheduling work?
back: A priority number is associated with each process, and the PCU is allocated the process with the highest priority (the smaller the integer, the higher the priority)

front: How does Round Robin work in CPU scheduling?
back: Each process gets a small unit of CPU time, and after this time has elapsed the process is pre-empted and added to the end of the ready queue.

front: What is a problem with priority scheduling, and what is a solution to this problem?
back: Starvation, low priority processes may never execute. The solution is to implement ageing, where the priority increases as time progresses.

front: How do soft real-time systems work?
back: Critical real-time tasks have the highest priority, but no guarantee as to when tasks will be scheduled.

front: What is response time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: The amount of time it takes from when a request was submitted until the first response is produced, not output

front: What does NUMA stand for?
back: non-uniform memory access

front: What is turnaround time? (CPU Scheduling)
back: Amount of time to execute a particular process

front: What is a solution to the problem of indefinite blockage of low-priority processes?
back: ageing

front: When does the CPU scheduling decisions take place?
back: When the CPU switches from running to waiting state, from running to ready state, from waiting to ready or terminates

front: Which is optimal, first come first serve or shortest-job-first scheduling?
back: Shortest-job-first

front: What is a cache miss?
back: accessing data that are not in cache memory

front: What is SMP?
back: symmetric multiprocessing

front: What is required to prevent race conditions when accessing shared kernel data structures?
back: mutex locks

front: When is the dispatcher invoked?
back: during every context switch

front: What is process contention scope?
back: Thread library schedules user-level threads to run on an LWP (many-to-one, many-to-many, models)

front: How do hard real-time systems work?
back: Task must be serviced by its deadline.

front: What is dispatch latency?
back: The time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another running.

front: What could be used to protect the common ready queue from a race condition?
back: locking

front: What is I/O burst?
back: Use of I/O by a process

front: What is contention scope?
back: How a user thread is mapped to a kernel thread

front: What is CPU burst?
back: Use of the CPU by a process

front: What is CPU-I/O burst cycle?
back: Process execution is composed of cycles of CPU and I/O bursts sequentially

front: What does the dispatcher module do?
back: Give control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

front: The contents of the file /proc/2166/status are what?
back: list various statistics for the process with pid = 2166

front: What is the convoy effect?
back: Queueing up of other processes while on e big CPU bound process is running with FCFS scheduling

front: What is the goal of CPU scheduling?
back: Switching the CPU among processes to increase productivity

front: What is a preemptive scheduler?
back: Processes can be interrupted while they are running in favor of running other processes (everything after windows 95 uses this)

front: How does earliest deadline first scheduling (EDF) work?
back: Priorities are assigned according to deadlines.  The deadline must be announced by the process with it becomes runnable.

front: What is a non-preemptive scheduler?
back: Once the CPU has been allocated to a process the process keeps the CPU until it releases the CPU by terminating or switches to ready state

front: What is event latency?
back: The amount of time that elapses from when an event occurs to when it is serviced.

front: What is the basic computational unit of a CPU?
back: a core

front: What is time quantom in round robin scheduling?
back: The slice of time that each process gets

front: What is the interval from the time of submission to completion of the process called?
back: turnaround time

front: What is the simplest CPU-scheduling algorithm?
back: first-come first-serve (FCFS) scheduling algorithm

front: The average waiting time under the FCFS policy is often how long?
back: Quite long

front: What is the difference between pre-emptive and non pre-emptive CPU scheduling?
back: In non-preemptive CPU scheduling algorithms the processes wait until they’re terminated, in preemptive each process is given a set time.

front: What is interrupt latency?
back: Time from arrival of interrupt to start of routine that services interrupt

front: What does the OS do to the CPU when a process has to wait?
back: the operating system takes the CPU away from that process and gives the CPU to another process

front: What is a critical section?
back: A section of the code that is critical, where only one process may be in the section at a time.

front: What is the entry section?
back: The section before the critical section, where each process must ask permission to enter critical section

front: What does it mean when a memory model is strongly ordered?
back: A memory modification of one processor is immediately visible to all other processors

front: What is a preemptive kernel?
back: Preemptive Kernel, as name suggests, is a type of kernel that always executes the highest priority task that are ready to run. It cannot use-non-reentrant functions unless and until functions are mutual exclusive.

front: What is a deadlock?
back: A deadlock is when two or more processes are waiting indefinitely for an event that can be caused by only one of the waiting processes.

front: What is memory model?
back: The memory guarantees a computer architecture makes to application programs

front: What problem do mutex locks solve?
back: Critical section problem

front: What refers to a set of properties that a system must satisfy to ensure that processes make progress during their execution life cycle?
back: Liveness

front: What does it mean when a memory model is weakly ordered?
back: A memory modification of one processor may not be immediately visible to all other processors

front: What is the exit section and remainder section?
back: The exit section is the section immediately following the critical section. The remainder section is the section following the exit section.

front: What is a non-preemptive kernel?
back: Non-Preemptive Kernel, as name suggests, is a type of kernel that is free from race conditions on kernel data structures as only one process is active in kernel at a time. It is considered as a serious drawback for real time applications as it does not allow preemption of process running in kernel mode.

front: A binary semaphore is the same as what other locking mechanism?
back: mutex locks

front: Peterson's solution provides a good algorithmic description of solving what problem?
back: critical-section problem

front: If two processes were to open files simultaneously, the separate updates to this list could result in what?
back: a race condition

front: On what system are spinlocks preferred?
back: multiprocessor

front: Who introduced semaphores?
back: Edsger Dijkstra

front: What is a binary semaphore?
back: An integer value that can range between only 0 and 1

front: What is a counting semaphore?
back: An integer value that can range over an unrestricted domain

front: What is special about atomic variables?
back: They provide atomic (uninterruptible) updates on basic data types such as integers and booleans.

front: Which is preferable, mutex locks or binary semaphores?
back: Mutex locks

front: What is a memory barrier?
back: An instruction that forces any change in memory to be made visible to all other processors

front: In the linux kernel, what can spinlocks be replaced by on single-cpu systems?
back: Enabling and disabling kernel preemption

front: Where is POSIX synchronization API widely used?
back: UNIX, Linux and macOS

front: What is the bounded buffer problem also known as?
back: Producer consumer problem

front: Was the Linux kernel preemptive or non-preemptive prior to version 2.6?
back: Non-preemptive.

front: What is the problem in reader-writer problem?
back: Problems occur when both readers and writers try to access the object, or when more than one writer tries to access.

front: How do we solve the reader-writer problem?
back: Using semaphores, so that when a writer is writing, only the writer has access to the file.

front: What is the bounded buffer problem a classic problem of?
back: Synchronization

front: How do we solve the bounded buffer problem?
back: By creating two counting semaphores "full" and "empty" to keep track of the current number of full and empty buffers respectively.

front: Why are functional programming languages good for concurrency?
back: They do not have a state, and therefore there are no issues in concurrency.

front: How can you invalidate hold and wait to ensure deadlock prevention?
back: Guarantee that whenever a process requests a resource, it does not hold any other resources.

front: As long as the state is safe, what can the operating system avoid?
back: unsafe (and deadlocked) states

front: What is banker’s algorithm?
back: The banker’s algorithm is a resource allocation and deadlock avoidance algorithm

front: A thread requests resources; if the resources are not available at that time, what state does the thread enter?
back: a waiting state

front: What is starvation?
back: Starvation is the problem that occurs when low priority processes get jammed for an unspecified time as the high priority processes keep executing.

front: What is a possibility if a thread needs several popular processes?
back: Starvation

front: What is another name for unsafe states?
back: deadlocked state

front: When can you invalidate mutual exclusion to ensure that deadlocks do not happen? (When is mutual exclusion not required)
back: Mutual Exclusion is not required for sharable resources (e.g. read-only files). Mutual exclusion must hold for non-sharable resources

front: What is liveness failure similar to?
back: deadlock

front: What is effective access time?
back: The ‘effective access time’ is essentially the (weighted) average time it takes to get a value from memory.

front: What is Contiguous Allocation?
back: Contiguous memory allocation is basically a method in which a single contiguous section/part of memory is allocated to a process or file needing it.

front: What is external fragmentation?
back: Total memory space exists to satisfy a request, but it is not contiguous.

front: What is internal fragmentation?
back: Allocated memory may be slightly larger than requested memory

front: What are Hierarchical Page Tables?
back: Multilevel Paging is a paging scheme which consist of two or more levels of page tables in a hierarchical manner.

front: What size is a frame? (In paging)
back: Of power 2 between 512 bytes and 16 Mbytes

front: What is paging?
back: Paging is a storage mechanism used to retrieve processes from the secondary storage into the main memory in the form of pages.

front: What is page offset used for?
back: It is used along with page number to define the physical memory address that is sent to the memory unit.

front: What does the Page-table base register and Page-table length register do?
back: PTBR points to the page table, PTLR indicates the size of the page table.

front: What is translation lookaside buffer?
back: A translation lookaside buffer (TLB) is a memory cache that stores recent translations of virtual memory to physical addresses for faster retrieval.

front: What happens on a translation lookaside buffer miss?
back: The value is loaded into the TLB for faster access next time.

front: What is the page number used for, and what does it contain?
back: It is used as an index into a page table, and it contains the base address of each page in physical memory

front: What does android do instead of swapping?
back: Terminates apps if low free memory, but first writes application state to flash for fast restart

front: What is a page table?
back: A page table is the data structure used by a virtual memory system in a computer operating system to store the mapping between virtual addresses and physical addresses.

front: What are Hashed Page Tables?
back: n hashed page tables, the virtual page number in the virtual address is hashed into the hash table. They are used to handle address spaces higher than 32 bits.

front: Where is page table kept?
back: In main memory.

front: What is swapping?
back: Swapping is a memory management scheme in which any process can be temporarily swapped from main memory to secondary memory so that the main memory can be made available for other processes.

front: What is variable-sized memory partitioning?
back: In variable-sized memory partitioning, the main memory is divided into blocks of the same or different sizes

front: What is Contiguous memory?
back: Consecutive blocks of memory allocated to user processes are called contiguous memory. For example, if a user process needs some x bytes of contiguous memory, then all the x bytes will reside in one place in the memory that is defined by a range of memory addresses: 0x0000 to 0x00FF

front: When is dynamic loading useful?
back: when large amounts of code are needed to handle infrequently occurring cases

front: What is the Inverted Page Table technique?
back: An inverted page table is one of the techniques to structure a page table, where the table is indexed by the actual frame number in the physical memory.

front: Address binding of instructions and data to memory addresses can happen at three different stages, what are the stages?
back: Compile time, load time and execution time

front: What is the difference between logical address (virtual address) and physical address?
back: The logical address is generated by the CPU and the physical address is seen by the memory unit.

front: What is MMU short for?
back: Memory-Management Unit

front: What is dynamic linking?
back: Dynamic linking is linking postponed until execution time.

front: What is static linking?
back: System libraries and program code combined by the loader into the binary program image.

front: What does paging allow a process's physical address space to be?
back: noncontiguous

front: What is a hole? (In memory)
back: A block of available memory

front: What is the advantage of dynamic loading?
back: a routine is loaded only when it is needed

front: What does iOS do instead of swapping?
back: It asks apps to voluntarily relinquish allocated memory

front: What is the purpose of the MMU (Memory Management Unit)?
back: It is a hardware device that at run time maps virtual to physical address

front: What is virtual address space?
back: Logical view of how process is stored in memory

front: What is a page fault?
back: An exception that the memory management unit raises when a process accesses a memory page that has not yet been brought into main memory.

front: What happens if the valid-invalid bit is invalid when the MMU is translating?
back: A page fault occurs

front: How is the valid-invalid bit on each page table entry initially set?
back: Initially it is set to I (invalid, not in memory)

front: What does it mean for a program to be memory-resident?
back: A memory-resident program has the ability to stay in the computer's memory after execution and to continuously run.

front: What is a swapper that deals with pages called?
back: A pager

front: What is pure demand paging?
back: When no pages are loaded into the memory initially.

front: What is virtual memory?
back: Separation of user logical memory from physical memory

front: What is slab allocation?
back: A memory management mechanism intended for efficient memory allocation of objects.

front: Why are page replacement algorithms needed?
back: Because we need to know which pages should be replaced when a new page comes in.

front: What is the buddy system?
back: A memory allocation algorithm that divides memory into partitions to try to satisfy a memory request as suitably as possible.

front: How does the buddy system allocate memory?
back: Using power-of-2 allocator, which satisfies requests in units sized as power of 2, and requests are rounded up to next highest power of 2.

front: What did SLUB replace as the default allocator for the Linux kernel?
back: SLAB

front: What does it mean when a page table entry is valid?
back: The program is in-memory, or memory resident.

front: What is demand paging?
back: Demand paging is a method of virtual memory management. In a system that uses demand paging, the operating system copies a disk page into physical memory only if an attempt is made to access it and that page is not already in memory.

front: What is a victim frame?
back: A frame which will be replaced by a frame replacement algorithm.

front: What does Copy-on-Write allow?
back: Copy-on-Write (COW) allows both parent and child processes to initially share the same pages in memory. If either process modifies a shared page, then the page is copied.

front: Is HDD best at random or sequential I/O?
back: Sequential.

front: When is the root partition mounted?
back: At boot time.

front: What is network-attached storage?
back: Storage made available over a network rather than over a local connection

front: What is disk bandwidth?
back: The total number of bytes transferred, divided by the total time between the first request for service and the completion of the last transfer

front: Why does NVM performance decrease with age?
back: Because of many erase cycles

front: What is a problem with the shortest seek time first algorithm?
back: Starvation may occur

front: How does the SCAN algorithm work?
back: The disk arm starts at one end of the disk and moves toward the other end, servicing requests until it gets to the other end of the disk, where the head movement is reversed and servicing continues.

front: How does the shortest seek time first (SSTF) algorithm work?
back: It selects the request with the minimum seek time from the current head position.

front: What is the difference between the C-SCAN and the SCAN algorithm?
back: When the head has moved to the end of the disk, in the C-SCAN algorithm it immediately goes back.

front: What is the SCAN algorithm sometimes called?
back: The elevator algorithm

front: Is NVM best at random or sequential I/O?
back: Random.

front: What is the general term for nonvolatile storage?
back: NVS

front: How is the life span of an SSD measured?
back: Drive writes per day (DWPD). Meaning a 1TB NAND drive with rating of 5DPWPD is expected to have 5TB per day written within warrantee period without failing

front: Where does NVMe connect to?
back: PCI bus directly

front: How does overwriting work on an SSD?
back: It does not.

front: What is NVM express?
back: electrical rather than mechanical

front: What does NVM stand for?
back: Nonvolatile memory

front: Why can NVM devices be more reliable than HDDs?
back: They have no moving parts and can be faster because they have no seek time or rotational latency

front: CDs, DVDs, and Blu-ray discs are what type of storage media?
back: removable

front: What is positioning time in HDDs? (random-access time)
back: The time it takes to move disk arm to desired cylinder (seek time) and time for desired sector to rotate under the disk head (rotational latency)

front: What is seek time in HDDs?
back: The time it takes to move disk arm to desired cylinder

front: What is a head crash in HDDs?
back: When the disk head makes contact with the disk surface (not good)

front: What are disk-drive like NVM devices called?
back: SSD’s

front: What is the transfer rate in HDDs?
back: The rate at which data flow between drive and computer

front: What is Fibre Channel (FC)?
back: A complex controller, usually separate circuit board plugging into bus

front: What does Vectored I/O allow a system call to do?
back: Vectored I/O allows one system call to perform multiple I/O operations

front: NFS, a common distributed file sharing method, is short for what?
back: Network File System

front: What does the open-file table do?
back: Tracks open files

front: What can be of two types of path names?
back: absolute and relative

front: What are tree-structured directories?
back: Directories grouped into trees

front: What is a two-level directory?
back: Separate directory for each user

front: What are partitions also known as?
back: Minidisks, slices

front: The simplest directory structure is what?
back: single-level directory

front: What are useful for files that are shared by several processes?
back: File locks

front: What is the file-open count?
back: Counter of number of times a file is open – to allow removal of data from open-file table when last processes closes it.

front: What is the difference between sequential access and direct access?
back: With sequential access you read and write the next byte, while in direct access you can read/write byte number n. For direct access the file is fixed length.

front: What is a single-level directory?
back: A single directory for all users. This presents us with a naming and grouping problem.

front: What is an unmounted file system mounted at?
back: A mount point

front: Repositioning within a file is also known as what?
back: a file seek

front: What is the file pointer?
back: The pointer to last read/write location, per process that has the file open

front: What is file locking?
back: a mechanism that restricts access to a computer file, or to a region of a file, by allowing only one user or process to modify or delete it at a specific time and to prevent reading of the file while it's being modified or deleted.

front: What is a link in a directory file system?
back: Another name (pointer) to an existing file

front: What is a device driver?
back: A device driver is a special kind of software program that controls a specific hardware device attached to a computer.

front: What are the most common strategies used to select a free hole from the set of available holes?
back: First fit and best fit

front: What does consistency checking do?
back: Compares data in directory structure with data blocks on disk, and tries to fix inconsistencies

front: What is NFS short for?
back: The Sun Network File System

front: What is NFS?
back: An implementation and a specification of a software system for accessing remote files across LANs or WANs

front: What does the root partition contain?
back: The OS

front: How do keys work in asymmetric encryption?
back: A public key and a private key are used together to encrypt

front: What is the term for people who can run pre-written exploit code to attack a given system?
back: Script kiddies

front: When can a code-injection attack occur?
back: When system code has bugs allowing executable code to be added or modified

front: What is the purpose of a Virus dropper?
back: It inserts a virus onto the system (usually a trojan horse)

front: How do keys work in symmetric encryption?
back: The same key is used to encrypt and decrypt

front: What are digital certificates used for?
back: A proof of who or what owns a public key.

front: What does a virus dropper do?
back: Inserts virus onto the system (usually a Trojan horse)

front: What is a digital signature?
back: a mathematical scheme for verifying the authenticity of digital messages or documents

front: What is ransomware?
back: A logical bomb that locks up data via encryption, demanding payment to unlock it

front: Who are certificate authorities?
back: Trusted parties that stores, signs, and issues digital certificates

front: What is a Trojan Horse?
back: A code segment that misuses its environment (e.g. spyware, pop-up browser windows, covert channels)

front: What is mandatory access control?
back: mandatory access control refers to a type of access control by which the operating system or database constrains the ability of a subject or initiator to access or generally perform some sort of operation on an object or target

front: What is audit trail?
back: A security-relevant chronological record, set of records, and/or destination and source of records that provide documentary evidence of the sequence of activities that have affected at any time a specific operation, procedure, or event.

front: What is principle of least privilege?
back: Programs, users and systems should be given just enough privileges to perform their tasks

front: What are protection rings?
back: mechanisms to protect data and functionality from faults and malicious behaviour. It puts parts of the operating system into different rings of privilege

front: Do usb mice and sound cards use character devices or block devices?
back: Character devices.

front: What does the minor number in /dev signify?
back: The minor number corresponds to the number of instances of the device. A minor number starts at 0 and goes to the number of instances minus one. Each instance will have its own minor number.

front: What is a race condition?
back: Race conditions are a result of uncontrolled access to shared data.

front: What is a spinlock?
back: A lock that forces a process that is trying to access the lock to wait in a loop while repeatedly checking if the lock is available, until the lock is available.

front: What is the consequence of a fault in a kernel module, and how is it different from a fault in a normal application?
back: In a normal program a debugger can catch faults, while with kernel modules the kernel, or maybe even the entire system fails completely.

front: What is the difference between insmod and modprobe?
back: Modprobe loads any other modules required by the module you want to load, but only look sin the standard installed modules directory.

front: Do kernel modules run in user space?
back: No, they run in kernel space. Normal applications run in user space.

front: What is locking or mutual exclusion?
back: A technique for access management that makes sure only one thread of execution can manipulate a shared resource at any time.

front: What does the major number in /dev signify?
back: A unique way of identifying a driver which can be associated with a particular device device.

front: What is the difference between kernel modules and applications?
back: Two main differences: Kernel Modules are exclusively event-driven and a kernel modules exit function always has to clean up from the init function.

front: What is the main difficulty that a programmer must overcome in writing an operating system for a real-time environment?
back: The main difficulty is keeping the operating system within the fixed time constraints of a real-time system. If the system does not complete a task in a certain time frame, it may cause a breakdown of the entire system.

front: What is the main disadvantage of the layered approach to system design?
back: Poor performance due to overhead of traversing through the different layers to obtain a service provided by the operating system

front: What is the main advantage of the layered approach to system design?
back: It is easier to debug and modify, because changes affect only limited sections of the system rather than touching all sections. Information is kept only where it is needed and is accesible only within a defined and restricted area, so any bugs affecting that data must be limited to a specific module or layer.

front: What is the purpose of system programs?
back: They can be thought of as bundles of useful system calls. They provide basic functionality to users so that users do not need to write their own programs to solve common problems.

front: What system calls have to be executed by a command interpreter or shell in order to start a new process on a UNIX system?
back: fork() system call and an exec() system call. Fork() clones the currently executing process, while exec() overlays a new process based on a different executable over the calling process

front: What is the purpose of the command interpreter? Why is it usually seperate from the kernel?
back: It reads commands from the user and executes them, usually by turning them into one or more system calls. It is not part of the kernel, because the interpreter is subject to changes.

front: What is the purpose of system calls?
back: They allow user-level processes to request services of the operating system

front: Why do some systems store the operating system in firmware, while others store it on disk?
back: For certain devices, such as embedded systems, a disk with a file system may not be available for the device. In this situation the OS must be stored in the firmware.

front: When a process creates a new process using the fork() operating, which of the following states is shared between the parent process and the child process? Stack,m heap and shared memory segments
back: Only the shared memory segments are shared between the parent process and the newly forked child process. Copies of the stack and the heap are made for the newly created process.

front: When each thread is performing the same task but on different data, is it data or task parallelism?
back: Data parellelism.

front: What resources are used when a thread is created? How do they differ from those used when a process is created?
back: Thread creation typically uses fewer resources than process creation. Creating a process requires allocating a process control block, a rather large data structure. Allocating and managing the memory map is typically the most time-consuming activity. Creating either a user thread or a kernel thread involves allocating a small data structure to hold a register set, stack and priority.

front: What are the actions taken by a kernel to context-switch between kernel-level threads?
back: It typically requires saving the value of the CPU registers from the thread being switched out and restoring the CPU registers of the new thread being scheduled.

front: Explain the difference between preemptive and nonpreemptive scheduling.
back: In preemptive scheduling, the CPU is allocated to the processes for a limited time whereas, in Non-preemptive scheduling, the CPU is allocated to the process till it terminates or switches to the waiting state.

front: A CPU scheduling algorithm determines an order for the execution of its scheduled processes. Given ‘n’ processes to be scheduled on one processor, how many possible different schedules are there?
back: n!

front: What advantage is there in having different time-quantum sizes at different levels of a multilevel queueing system?
back: Processes that need more frequent servicing, for instance, interactive processes such as editors, can be in a queue with a small time quantum.

front: What is the difference between volatile and nonvolatile storage?
back: volatile memory forgets its contents if its power source is removed

front: What is the common name used to refer to the operating system program?
back: Kernel

front: What is another term for multiprocessor system?
back: Parallel systems/multicore systems

front: The most common multiple-processor system uses asymmetric multiprocessing. (True/False)
back: False - Symmetric Multiprocessing is the most common.

front: What are the four components of a computer system?
back:
• Hardware
  • Operating System
  • Application Programs
  • Users

front: What are the two different approaches for providing a user interface?
back: Via the CLI or via the GUI

front: (True/False) Performance Tuning is a type of debugging
back: It is true.

front: What is the name of the small piece of code that locates the kernel and loads it into memory?
back: Bootstrap program / bootstrap loader

front: What does the term SYSGEN refer to?
back: System generation; the process of configuring or generating an instance of an operating system for each individual computer site/architecture.

front: List at least three operating system function that maintain efficient operation of the system.
back: Resource allocation, accounting, protection and security

front: Provide an example of an operating system that uses the simple structure.
back: MS-DOS operating system

front: List at least two different hybrid operating systems.
back: iOS, Android, macOS

front: (True/False) Dtrace is available for most Windows systems.
back: False, it has been added to macOS and FreeBSD

front: What is the difference between policy and mechanism?
back: Mechanisms determine hwo to do something; policies determine what will be done.

front: (True/False) The view of must users see of the oeprating system is defined by application and system programs rather than system calls
back: It is true

front: List at least three of the categories of system programs.
back: File management, status information, file modification, programming-language support, program loading and execution, communications, ba ckground services.

front: What is a program that has been loaded and executing called?
back: A process

front: List at least three of the major categories of system calls.
back: Process Control, File Manipulation, Device Manipulation, Information Maintenance, Communications, Protection

front: List at least three different ways for structuring an operating system.
back:
Simple structure (MS-DOS & early UNIX)
Layered Approach
Microkernel (Tru64 UNIX)
Modules (Linux, Solaris, Windows)
Hybrid Structure (Mac OS X)

front: What is a system call?
back: A mechanism that provides the interface between a process and the operating system.

front: What are the two types of pipes?
back: Ordinary pipes, named pipes

front: Are UDP sockets connection-oriented or connection-less?
back: Connection-less

front: What are the four components of a process?
back: Stack, heap, data, text (and program counter, processor register)

front: What is another term for process?
back: Thread or job

front: (True/False) Most operating systems allow a process to have multiple threads
back: It is true

front: What is the degree of multiprogramming?
back: The number of processes in memory

front: What is the term that describes saving the state of one process, and restoring the state of another?
back: Context switching

front: Are TCP sockets conncetion-oriented or connection-less?
back: Connection-oriented

front: Provide at least two types of communication mechanisms in client-server systems
back: Sockets, Pipes, Remote Procedure Calls

front: (True/False) Message passing is typically faster than shared memory
back: False, shared memory is faster for single-core, message passing shown to exhibit better performance for multi-core systems

front: What are the two system calls used with message-passing systems?
back: send() receive()

front: What are the two fundamental models of interprocess communication?
back: Shared memory and message passing

front: What is the name of the process that UNIX and Linux systems assign as the new parent of orphan processes?
back: Init

front: What is more common currently, assymetric or symmetric multiprocessing?
back: Symmetric multiprocessing, each processor is self scheduling opposed to assymetric where one processor does the scheduling for all others

front: What are the two types of burst that CPU schedulers are designed around?
back: CPU burst and I/O burst

front: List at least three different critea for designing a CPU scheduling algorithm
back:
CPU Utilization, keeping the CPU as busy as possible
Throughput, the number of processes completed per time unit
Turnaround time, how long it takes too execute a process
Waiting time, the sum of peroids spent waiting in the ready queue
Response time, time from submission of request to first response

front: What scheduling algorithm assigns the Cpu to the process that first requested it?
back: First-come, first-served

front: What scheduling algorithm assigns the CPU to a process for only its time slice (or time quantum)?
back: Round-robin scheduling

front: What scheduling algorithm assigns the CPU to the process with the shortest burst
back: Shortest-job-first

front: What are the two general hardware instructions that can be performed atomically?
back: test_and_set(), compare_and_swap()

front: (True/False) Under preemptive scheduling, when a process switches from the running to the ready state, it may lose control of the CPU
back: It is false

front: What are the two forms of processor affinity?
back:
Soft affinity, when an operating system has a policy of attempting to keep a process running on the same processor but cannot guarantee it
Hard affinity, allowing a process to specifiy a subset of processors on which it can run

front: What is push migration?
back: A specific task periodically checks the load on each processor and if it finds an imblanace it then evenly distributes the load on each processor

front: What is pull migration?
back: When an idle processor pulls a waiting task from a busy processor

front: What are two general types of real-time scheduling?
back: Soft real-time systems and hard real-time systems

front: What real-time scheduling algorithm uses deadline as its scheduling criteria?
back: Hard real-time systems

front: What real-time scheduling algorithm is used for scheduling periodic tasks with static priorities?
back: Rate-monotonic

front: What scheduling algorithm assigns the CPU to the process with the highest priority?
back: Priority scheduling

front: What are the two general approaches for load balancing?
back: Push migration and pull migration.

front: What is the prupose of the dispatcher?
back: It gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler

front: What is the dispatcher latency?
back: The time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one processor and start another

front: What is bounded waiting?
back: After a process makes a request for getting into its critical section, there is a limit for how many other processes can get into their critical section, before this process's request is granted

front: What is special about a preemptive kernel?
back: It allows preemption of process when running in kernel mode

front: What is special about a non-preemptive kernel?
back: It runs until it exits kernel mode, blocks, or voluntarily yields CPU, therefore free of race conditions

front: (True/False) test_and_set is executed atomically.
back: It is true

front: What is an atomic variable?
back: A variable that provides atomic updates on basic data types such as integers and booleans

front: A spinlock requires what type of waiting?
back: Busy waiting

front: What is the domain of a counting semaphore?
back: Unrestricted

front: What is the domain of a binary semaphore?
back: 0 and 1

front: Using the semaphore operations signal() and wait(), what is the only valid order?
back: wait(mutex), signal(mutex)

front: What is priority inversion?
back: Scheduling problem when lower-priority process holds a lock needed by a higher-priority process

front: How do you solve priority inversion?
back: Via priority-inhertiance protocol

front: What does the priority inheritance protocol allow?
back: The priority of the highest thread waiting to access a shared resource to be assigned to the thread currently using the resource.

front: If the current value of counter = 5, what are its possible values if the producer and consumer processes run concurrently?
back: Either 5, 6, or 7.

front: What is the term for describing the situation where shared data may be manipulated concurrently and the outcome of the execution depends upon the order of access?
back: Race Condition

front: What is the term used to describe the segment of code where shared data is accessed and possibly manipulated?
back: Critical section

front: What are the three requirements a solution to the critical-section problem must satisfy?
back: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, and bounded waiting.

front: What is progress?
back: If no process is executing in its critical section and some processes wish to enter their critical sections, then only those processes that are not executing in their remainder section can participate in deciding which will enter its critical section next, and this selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.

front: What is bounded waiting?
back: There exists a bound, or limit, on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections after a process has made request to enter its critical section and before that request is granted.

front: What is following a description of? This allows a process ot be temporarily interrupted without requiring its cooperation, with the intention of resuming the task at a later time while it is running in kernel mode
back: Preemptive kernel.

front: (True/False) A nonpreemptive kernel is essentially free form race conditions.
back: This is true, as only one process is active in the kernel at a time

front: (True/False) There are no guarantees that Peterson’s solution works correctly on modern computer architectures.
back: True, Peterson’s solution works correctly for architectures with single CPU, but many modern architectures involve CPUs with multicores or multiprocessors.

front: (True/False) All solutions to the critical section problem are based on locking.
back: This is true.

front: What are the two general hardware instructions that can be performed atomically?
back: test_and_set(), compare_and_swap()

front: What are the two functions used with mutex locks?
back: acquire() and release()

front: (True/False) A spinlock is a type of mutex lock.
back: True, it is basically a mutex lock with busy waiting.

front: True or False? Semaphores can provide the same functionality as mutex locks.
back: True, binary semaphores are very similar to mutex locks.

front: What are the two operations that can be performed on a semaphore?
back: signal() and wait()

front: (True/False) A binary semaphore is functionally equivalent to a mutex lock.
back: True.

front: What are the two operations that can be performed on a condition variable?
back: signal() and wait()

front: Name at least one modern programming language that has incorporated the idea of a monitor.
back: Java, C#

front: (True/False) The system model for deadlocks first requires a process request a resource, then use the resource, and finally release the resource.
back: This is true

front: What are the four necessary conditions for characterizing deadlock?
back: Mutual Exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption and circular set. Therefore we can also prevent a deadlock by preventing any of these conditions.

front: What is the only reasonable condition that can be used to prevent deadlocks from occurring?
back: Circular-wait. Impose a total ordering of all resource types, and require that each process requests resources in an increasing order of enumeration.

front: Why can disabling interrupts frequently affect the system’s clock?
back: The clock is updated at every clock interrupt, so disabling interrupts could cause the clock to miss an interrupt and end up being behind.

front: Why are spinlocks not appropriate for single-processor systems, yet often used in multiprocessor systems?
back: You can only break a proces sout of a spinlock by executing a different process.
